sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
Before you enable Encryption at Rest using AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) over PrivateLink,
review the following use cases, benefits, limitations, and prerequisites.

sections.heading: Use Case
sections.body:
Suppose your Atlas deployment is on a single cloud service provider.
You now have a requirement that all access to your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) occurs over your
cloud provider's private networking infrastructure. This page walks you
through the steps to enable private endpoint connections for your
Atlas project.

sections.heading: Benefits
sections.body:
You can configure
Encryption at Rest with AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) over private networking. This configuration allows all
traffic to your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) to pass through a set of private endpoints and avoids
exposing your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) to the public internet or public IP addresses. This configuration also
eliminates the need to maintain allowed IP addresses while enhancing
data security by keeping all AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) traffic within AWS (Amazon Web Services)'s private
network.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
- Atlas supports Encryption at Rest using AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) over
  private networking only for single-cloud deployments.

- Atlas supports Encryption at Rest using AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) over
  private networking only for projects in the `ACTIVE` state.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
To enable customer-managed keys with AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) for a MongoDB
project, you must:

- Use an M10 or larger cluster.

* Have an AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role with sufficient privileges. Atlas must
  have permission to perform the following actions with your key:

  - [DescribeKey](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_DescribeKey.html)

  - [Encrypt](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_Encrypt.html)

  - [Decrypt](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_Decrypt.html)

  > **Note:** If you wish to use the AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key with an AWS IAM role from a
  > different AWS account instead of that of the IAM role which
  > created the AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key , ensure you have sufficient privileges:
  >
  > - Add a key policy statement under the AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key  to include the
  >   external AWS account.
  >
  > - Add an IAM inline policy for the IAM role in the external AWS
  >   account.
  >
  > For a comprehensive discussion of IAM roles and customer master
  > keys, see the [AWS documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policy-modifying-external-accounts.html).
  >
  > After confirming the above privileges, you can follow the usual
  > steps to configure the KMS settings in Atlas, with the
  > following exception:
  >
  > - You must provide the full ARN (Amazon Resource Name) for
  >   the AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key  (e.g. `arn:aws:kms:eu-west-2:111122223333:key/12345678-1234-1234-1234-12345678`)
  >   instead of the master key ID (e.g. `12345678-1234-1234-1234-12345678`)
  >   in the AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key ID field.

  To learn how to create an IAM (Identity and Access Management) role, see
  [IAM Roles](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html)
  in the AWS (Amazon Web Services) documentation.

  Atlas uses the same IAM (Identity and Access Management) role and AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key settings for
  all clusters in a project for which Encryption at Rest is enabled.

* If your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) configuration requires it, [allow
  access](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/conditions-aws.html#conditions-aws-ip-address)
  from [Atlas IP addresses](atlas-add-inbound-ips) and the public IP addresses or DNS hostnames of your cluster nodes so that Atlas
  can communicate with your KMS (Key Management Service). You must include the IP addresses in your [managed IAM role policy](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_manage-edit.html) by
  configuring [IP address condition operators](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_elements_condition_operators.html#Conditions_IPAddress) in your policy document.
  If the node IP addresses [Do Atlas cluster's public IPs ever change?](faq-public-ip-changes), you must update your
  configuration to avoid connectivity interruptions.

sections.heading: Procedures
Enable Role-Based Access for Your Encryption Key for a Project
sections.body:
Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

After you enable role-based access for your encryption key for your project,
enable private endpoint connections for your project by following
[Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](aws-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint).

sections.heading: Enable Role-Based Access for Your Encryption Key for a Project
sections.body:
To create a new AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role for accessing your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS keys for
encryption at rest, follow the [Set Up Unified AWS Access](set-up-unified-aws-access) procedure. If you have an existing
AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role that you want to authorize, follow the [Set Up Unified AWS Access](set-up-unified-aws-access)
procedure.

> **Note:** This policy statement allows MongoDB's AWS (Amazon Web Services) Principal to use the
> customer's KMS key for encryption and decryption operations. The
> Atlas Principal is not secret and is used across all Atlas
> customers. This is a highly-restricted, purpose-limited AWS (Amazon Web Services)
> account, with no resources in it other than the IAM user. The
> `ExternalId` in the policy statement is unique for each Atlas
> project, but it is not secret. The `ExternalId` is used to
> mitigate the possibility of cross-context (confused deputy)
> vulnerabilities. Atlas's use of a common principal to access all
> customers' keys is an access pattern recommended by Amazon, as
> described [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_create_for-user_externalid.html).

The access policy for encryption at rest looks similar to the following:

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "kms:Decrypt",
        "kms:Encrypt",
        "kms:DescribeKey"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/12x345y6-7z89-0a12-3456-xyz123456789"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

- Select the role to assign from the AWS IAM role dropdown
  list.

- Specify your encryption key in the Customer Master Key ID
  field.

- Select the AWS (Amazon Web Services) region for your encryption key.

sections.heading: Enable Role-Based Access for Your Encryption Key for a Project
sections.body:
Use the [API](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2)
endpoint to
create a new AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role. Atlas will use this role for
authentication with your AWS (Amazon Web Services) account.

Keep the returned field values `atlasAWSAccountArn` and
`atlasAssumedRoleExternalId` handy for use in the next step.

- Log in to your AWS (Amazon Web Services) Management Console.

- Navigate to the Identity and Access Management (IAM) service.

- Select Roles from the left-side navigation.

- Click on the existing IAM role you wish to use for Atlas access
  from the list of roles.

- Select the Trust Relationships tab.

- Click the Edit trust relationship button.

- Edit the Policy Document. Add a new `Statement` object
  with the following content.

  > **Note:** This policy statement allows MongoDB's AWS (Amazon Web Services) Principal to use the
  > customer's KMS key for encryption and decryption operations. The
  > Atlas Principal is not secret and is used across all Atlas
  > customers. This is a highly-restricted, purpose-limited AWS (Amazon Web Services)
  > account, with no resources in it other than the IAM user. The
  > `ExternalId` in the policy statement is unique for each Atlas
  > project, but it is not secret. The `ExternalId` is used to
  > mitigate the possibility of cross-context (confused deputy)
  > vulnerabilities. Atlas's use of a common principal to access all
  > customers' keys is an access pattern recommended by Amazon, as
  > described [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_create_for-user_externalid.html).

  > **Note:** Replace the highlighted lines with values returned from the API
  > call in step 1.

  ```json
  {
    "Version": "2020-03-17",
    "Statement": [
      {
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Principal": {
          "AWS": "<atlasAWSAccountArn>"
        },
        "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
        "Condition": {
          "StringEquals": {
            "sts:ExternalId": "<atlasAssumedRoleExternalId>"
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

- Click the Update Trust Policy button.

Use the [API](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2)
endpoint to authorize and configure the new IAM Assumed Role ARN (Amazon Resource Name).
If the API call is successful, you can use the `roleId` value when
configuring Atlas services that use AWS (Amazon Web Services).

Send a `PATCH` request to the [encryptionAtRest](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2) API endpoint
to update the `awsKms.roleId` field with your authorized AWS IAM
role ID.

> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

> **Example:** 
>
> ```none
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
>   --header "Accept: application/json" \
>   --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
>   --include \
>   --request PATCH \
>   "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v1.0/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest?pretty=true&envelope=true" \
>   --data '
>    {
>        "awsKms": {
>            "enabled": true,
>            "roleId": "<roleId>",
>            "customerMasterKeyID": "<master-key-id>",
>            "region": "<aws-region>"
>        }
>    }'
> ```

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
  --header "Accept: application/json" \
  --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --include \
  --request PATCH \
  "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v1.0/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest?pretty=true&envelope=true" \
  --data '
   {
       "awsKms": {
           "enabled": true,
           "roleId": "<roleId>",
           "customerMasterKeyID": "<master-key-id>",
           "region": "<aws-region>"
       }
   }'

sections.heading: Switch to Role-Based Access for Your Encryption Key for a Project
sections.body:
AWS (Amazon Web Services) requires the use of IAM (Identity and Access Management) *roles* instead of IAM (Identity and Access Management)
*users* for managing access to AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) encryption keys within Atlas.
If you initially configured your project to use IAM (Identity and Access Management) *user* credentials to access
AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) keys, switch to role-based access using the following procedure.

> **Important:** If you switch your encryption keys to role-based access, you can't
> undo the role-based access configuration and revert to
> credentials-based access for encryption keys on that project.

Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

sections.heading: Switch to Role-Based Access for Your Encryption Key for a Project
sections.body:
To create a new AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role for accessing your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS keys for
encryption at rest, follow the [Set Up Unified AWS Access](set-up-unified-aws-access) procedure. If you have an existing
AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role that you want to authorize, follow the [Set Up Unified AWS Access](set-up-unified-aws-access)
procedure.

sections.heading: Switch to Role-Based Access for Your Encryption Key for a Project
sections.body:
To update your encryption key management with the
Atlas Administration API, use the same steps outlined in the
[role-based access to your encryption key](aws-kms-enable-project).

sections.heading: Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
To enable private networking and set up a private
endpoint in your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service), you must do the following:

Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

After you enable customer key management for your project and set up private endpoints,
enable customer key management for each Atlas cluster in your project by following
[Enable Customer Key Management for an Atlas Cluster](aws-pvt-enable-cluster-encryption-at-rest).

> **Important:** If you have an existing multi-region AWS cluster with customer key management
> enabled using an AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) single-region key, you can't enable
> the **Require Private Networking** option.
> This configuration is not supported.

sections.heading: Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
On the Advanced page, in the Encryption at Rest
using your Key Management section, do the following steps:

- Expand Network Settings for your Azure Key Vault
  if it's collapsed.

- Toggle on the button next to Require Private
  Networking.

- Click Set up.

  The Set Up Private Networking for AWS page displays.

* Sign into your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) dashboard.

* In the Atlas UI, click Copy to save your customer master key ID.

* In your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) dashboard, replicate your key in all desired regions.
  To learn more, see the [AWS documentation](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-replicate.html#create-replica-keys).

* In the Atlas UI, click Continue

- Select the AWS (Amazon Web Services) regions from the dropdown.

- Click Continue.

Atlas automatically creates private endpoints in these
regions to allow you to connect by using private networking.

Atlas can take up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. The private endpoint can have one of the following
statuses:

| ACTIVE | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it. |
| ------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| FAILED | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                          |

sections.heading: Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
Send a `PATCH` request to the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updateencryptionatrest) and set the `requirePrivateNetworking`
flag value to `true`.

To learn more about the required parameters, see
[Update Configuration for Encryption at Rest using Customer-Managed Keys for One Project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updateencryptionatrest).

> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

> **Example:** 
>
> ```shell
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --include \
> --request PATCH "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/" \
> --data '
>   {
>     "awsKms": {
>       "accessKeyID": "019dd98d94b4bb778e7552e4",
>       "customerMasterKeyID": "string",
>       "enabled": true,
>       "region": "US_EAST_1",
>       "roleId": "32b6e34b3d91647abb20e7b8",
>       "secretAccessKey": "string",
>       "requirePrivateNetworking": true
>     }
>   }'
> ```

Use the Atlas Administration API to create a private endpoint to
communicate with your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service).

Send a `POST` request to the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-createencryptionatrestprivateendpoint) with the AWS (Amazon Web Services)
region that you want Atlas to create the private
endpoint in. You must send a separate request for each region
that you want Atlas to create a private endpoint in.

> **Example:** 
>
> ```shell
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --include \
> --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/privateEndpoints" \
> --data '
>   {
>     "regionName": "US_EAST_1"
>   }'
> ```

After you create the private endpoint, the following restrictions
apply:

- Atlas creates all new clusters only in the regions with
  approved private endpoints.

- Atlas deploys additional nodes for existing clusters only
  in the regions with approved private endpoints.

To check the status of the private endpoint, send a `GET`
request to the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoints).

> **Example:** **Input:**
>
> ```shell
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --include \
> --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/privateEndpoints"
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```shell
> {
>   "links": [
>     {
>       "href": "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas",
>       "rel": "self"
>     }
>   ],
>   "results": [
>     {
>       "cloudProvider": "AWS",
>       "id": "24-hexadecimal-digit-string",
>       "privateEndpointConnectionName": "string",
>       "regionName": "US_EAST_1",
>       "status": "INITIATING",
>     }
>   ],
>   "totalCount": 1
> }
> ```

Atlas can take up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. The private endpoint can have one of the following
statuses:

| ACTIVE | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it. |
| ------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| FAILED | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                          |

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
--header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
--header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
--include \
--request PATCH "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/" \
--data '
  {
    "awsKms": {
      "accessKeyID": "019dd98d94b4bb778e7552e4",
      "customerMasterKeyID": "string",
      "enabled": true,
      "region": "US_EAST_1",
      "roleId": "32b6e34b3d91647abb20e7b8",
      "secretAccessKey": "string",
      "requirePrivateNetworking": true
    }
  }'

sections.heading: Enable Customer Key Management for an Atlas Cluster
sections.body:
After you [Enable Role-Based Access for Your Encryption Key for a Project](aws-kms-pvt-enable-project), you must enable customer key
management for each Atlas cluster that contains data that you want
to encrypt.

> **Note:** You must have the [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) role to
> enable customer key management for clusters in that project.

For multi-region AWS CMK (customer-managed key), make sure to add **every** regional key ARN to the KMS key policy so that Atlas
clusters in those regions can access it.

For new clusters, toggle the
[Manage Your Own Encryption Keys](create-cluster-enable-encryption)
setting to **Yes** when you create the cluster.

For existing clusters:

For the cluster that contains data that you want to encrypt, click the
, then select Edit Configuration.

- Expand the Additional Settings panel.

- Toggle the Manage your own encryption keys setting to
  Yes.

- Verify the status of the Require Private Networking
  setting for your cluster.

  If you configured Encryption at Rest Using CMK (customer-managed key) (Over Private
  Networking) for Atlas at the project level, the status is
  Active. If you haven't configured any private
  endpoint connection for your project, the status is
  Inactive.

* Click Review Changes.

* Review your changes, then click Apply Changes to update
  your cluster.

sections.heading: Disable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project
sections.body:
To disable CMK (customer-managed key) for a project, you must first [remove](aws-remove-reject-pvt-endpoint) all private endpoints associated
with the project, regardless of their state. Atlas displays an error if
you attempt to disable CMK (customer-managed key) for a project that is associated with
active private endpoints.

After you remove all private endpoints for a project, you must disable
customer key management on each cluster in the project before you
disable the feature for the project.

> **Warning:** Do *not* disable or delete any AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) keys that any cluster in your
> Atlas project uses before you have disabled customer key
> management within the Atlas project. If Atlas can't access
> an AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key, any data that the key encrypted becomes inaccessible.

sections.heading: Create New Endpoints for a Project
sections.body:
After you enable and set up private endpoint connections for your
project, you can add additional endpoints at anytime from the
Atlas UI and the Atlas Administration API.

Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

sections.heading: Create New Endpoints for a Project
sections.body:
- Select the AWS (Amazon Web Services) regions from the dropdown.

- Click Continue.

Atlas automatically creates private endpoints in these
regions to allow you to connect by using private networking.

Atlas can take up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. The private endpoint can have one of the following
statuses:

| ACTIVE | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it. |
| ------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| FAILED | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                          |

sections.heading: Create New Endpoints for a Project
sections.body:
Use the Atlas Administration API to create a private endpoint to
communicate with your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service).

Send a `POST` request to the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-createencryptionatrestprivateendpoint) with the AWS (Amazon Web Services)
region in which you want Atlas to create the private
endpoint. You must send a separate request for each region
in which you want Atlas to create a private endpoint.

> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

> **Example:** 
>
> ```shell
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --include \
> --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/privateEndpoints" \
> --data '
>   {
>     "regionName": "US_EAST_1"
>   }'
> ```

After you create the private endpoint, the following restrictions
apply:

- Atlas creates all new clusters only in the regions with
  approved private endpoints.

- Atlas deploys additional nodes for existing clusters only
  in the regions with approved private endpoints.

To check the status of the private endpoint, send a `GET`
request to the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoints).

> **Example:** **Input:**
>
> ```shell
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-11-13+json" \
> --include \
> --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/privateEndpoints"
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```shell
> {
>   "links": [
>     {
>       "href": "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas",
>       "rel": "self"
>     }
>   ],
>   "results": [
>     {
>       "cloudProvider": "AWS",
>       "id": "24-hexadecimal-digit-string",
>       "privateEndpointConnectionName": "string",
>       "regionName": "US_EAST_1",
>       "status": "INITIATING",
>     }
>   ],
>   "totalCount": 1
> }
> ```

Atlas can take up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. The private endpoint can have one of the following
statuses:

| ACTIVE | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it. |
| ------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| FAILED | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                          |

sections.heading: Reject or Remove Private Endpoint Connection
sections.body:
You can remove private endpoint connections from the Atlas UI and
the Atlas Administration API.

Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

sections.heading: Reject or Remove Private Endpoint Connection
sections.body:
- Click the  icon in the Actions
  column for the private endpoint that you want to remove.

- Click Delete to confirm deletion of the private
  endpoint.

sections.heading: Reject or Remove Private Endpoint Connection
sections.body:
To successfully remove a private endpoint, send a `DELETE` request to
the Atlas Administration API [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-requestencryptionatrestprivateendpointdeletion) and specify the ID of
the project and of the private endpoint that you want to delete. You can
retrieve the ID of the private endpoint that you want to delete by sending
a `GET` request to the Atlas Administration API [Return One Private
Endpoint Service for One Provider](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupprivateendpointservice) endpoint.

> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

> **Example:** 
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin       API
> --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
> --include \
> --request DELETE "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/      privateEndpoints/{endpointId}" \
> --data '
>   {
>     "cloudProvider": "AWS",
>     "regions": [
>       "string"
>     ]
>   }'
> ```

When you use the Atlas Administration API to delete a private endpoint, the
private endpoint transitions to the `DELETING` status while Atlas
deletes the private endpoint.

If you remove or reject an active private endpoint from the AWS (Amazon Web Services) UI,
Atlas automatically attempts to recreate a new private endpoint in
the same region.

While Atlas attempts to create a new private endpoint, the status of
the private endpoint that you rejected or removed transitions to
`PENDING_RECREATION` and the new endpoint that Atlas attempts to
create is in `INITIATING` state. You must approve the new private
endpoint after it is created.

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
--header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin       API
--header "Content-Type: application/json" \
--include \
--request DELETE "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/      privateEndpoints/{endpointId}" \
--data '
  {
    "cloudProvider": "AWS",
    "regions": [
      "string"
    ]
  }'

sections.heading: View Private Endpoints and their Statuses
sections.body:
You can view the private endpoints in the various regions and their
statuses from the Atlas UI and the Atlas Administration API.

Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

Each private endpoint can be in one of the following statuses:

| ACTIVE | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it. |
| ------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| FAILED | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                          |

sections.heading: View Private Endpoints and their Statuses
sections.body:
You can view the private endpoint and their statuses from the
Atlas Administration API by sending a `GET` request to the
Atlas Administration API `encryptionAtRest`
[get all](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoints)
endpoint or [get one](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoint)
endpoint, for which you must specify the ID of the private endpoint
in the path.

> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

> **Example:** 
>
> Return All Private Endpoints for One Project
>
> ```
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin API
> --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
> --include \
> --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/privateEndpoints/"
> ```

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
--header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin API
--header "Content-Type: application/json" \
--include \
--request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AWS/privateEndpoints/"

sections.heading: Disable Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
To disable private endpoint connections for a project, you must first
[remove](aws-remove-reject-pvt-endpoint) all private endpoints,
associated with the project, regardless of their state. Atlas
doesn't disable private endpoint connections for a project if the
project is associated with active private endpoints.

After you remove all private endpoints for a project, you can disable
private endpoint connections for the project by using the Atlas UI
and the Atlas Administration API.

Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

sections.heading: Disable Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
- Under Encryption at Rest using your Key Management, select Azure Key Vault.

- Under Network Settings, toggle the button next to Require Private
  Networking to  Off.

- Click Save.

sections.heading: Disable Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
To disable a private endpoint connection, send a
`PATCH` request to the [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updateencryptionatrest) with the `requirePrivateNetworking` boolean
flag value set to `false`.

> **Example:** 
>
> ```shell
> {
>   "awsKms": {
>     "accessKeyID": "019dd98d94b4bb778e7552e4",
>     "customerMasterKeyID": "string",
>     "enabled": true,
>     "region": "US_EAST_1",
>     "roleId": "32b6e34b3d91647abb20e7b8",
>     "secretAccessKey": "string"
>     "requirePrivateNetworking": false
>   }
> }
> ```

sections.heading: Related Topics
sections.body:
- To enable Encryption at Rest using your Key Management when deploying
  an Atlas cluster, see [Manage Your Own Encryption Keys](create-cluster-enable-encryption).

- To enable Encryption at Rest using your Key Management for an
  existing Atlas cluster, see
  [Encryption at Rest](scale-cluster-enable-encryption).

- To learn more about Encryption at Rest using your Key Management in
  Atlas, see [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption).

- To learn more about MongoDB Encryption at Rest, see
  [Encryption at Rest](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest) in
  the MongoDB server documentation.

- To learn more about Encryption at Rest with Cloud Backups, see [Storage Engine and Cloud Backup Encryption](encrypted-cloud-provider-snapshot).

- To enable customer key Management for Search Nodes, see [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption).
